title: practical english usage - Part 70
content:
Was/were able to is also possible, especially to talk about people’ s ability.

It could be quite frightening if you were alone in our big old house.

My last car could do lθθmph.  In those days everybody could find a job.

My grandmother could sing like an angel.

She could read when she was four.

We use could to talk about general ability in the past.

3 past:   She    could      read     when      she    was     four.

Anybody who wants to can join the club.

‘ What can we do?* ‘ We can try asking Lucy for help.' we can forget all about it.

There are three possibilities: we can go to the police, we can talk to a lawyer, or I can't come out this evening: I have to see my brother.

We can go to Paris this weekend, because I don `t have to work.

circumstances that we  are in - what is possible in the situation.

We   also use can to say what we  are able (or unable) to do because of the 2 possible      in  the   situation:      We    can    go   to   Paris.

Henry is able to lift 100 kilos.

about people's ability.

Be able to (►  85) is used with similar meanings, especially when we  are talking Henry can lift 100 kilos.         My car can do 150mph.

Dogs can't climb trees.          Can gases freeze?

I can read Italian, but I can't speak it. These roses can grow anywhere.

their knowledge, skill, strength, nature, design, etc.

We   use can to say what people and things are able (or unable) to do because of 1 knowledge,        skill,   strength,      etc:  I can    read     Italian.

82 ability:    can   and   could Section Modal    Auxiliary      Verbs          7 grammar •  82  ability: can and could but not It could rain later this evening, perhaps, (          ll-ean-rain-late^...) However, could is possible in this sense.

'Where's Sarah?* 'She may be with Joe. (     She ean be.. .)* not We may go camping this summer,  (     We can go...) actually happen, or is actually happening. Instead, we  use may (►  71).

We   do not use can to talk about the chances (probability) that something will 7 chances:     Will     it  happen?        / Is  it  happening?            - can    not   used so we decided not to go.

We could have spent today at the beach, but we thought it was going to rain He could have been Prime Minister now if he hadn't decided to leave politics.

have not been realised.

This structure can also refer to present situations which  were possible but which 'Who sent those flowers?' 'I'm not sure. It could have been your mother.* to have happened (like may/might have, ►    71.8).

The  structure is sometimes used to talk about past events which  are not certain I couldn't have enjoyed myself more - it was a perfect day.

I couldn't have won, so I didn't go in for the race.

something even if they had wanted or tried to.

Negative sentences suggest that somebody would not have been able to do You could have helped me - why did you just sit and watch?

This structure can be used to criticise people for not doing things.

I could have won the race if I hadn't fallen.

Why did you jump  out of the window? You could have hurt yourself.

1 was so angry I could have killed her!

/ could have married anybody I wanted to.

could have + past participle something was possible, but did not happen.

to say that somebody was able to do something, but did not do it; or that We   use a special structure to talk about unrealised past ability or opportunities - 6 could      have      ...

'Can you help me?* 'What did you say?* 'I asked if you could help me.* Could is used in past indirect speech, when can was used in direct speech.

You could get a better job if you spoke a foreign language.

Could can mean 'would be able to’ .

When you're in Spain, you could go and see Alex.

'What shall we do tomorrow?* 'Well, we could go fishing.* Could is not only past: we  also use it as a 'softer’ , less definite form of can.

5  other     uses    of  could grammar •  83   ability: advanced points ' What are we going to eat?*' We could always warm up that soup.' him  a gift card.' 7 don't know what to get Mark for his birthday.1 'Well, you can always give Can/could always can mean ‘ can/could ... if there is nothing better’ .
